title: The connection between TMJ and unspoken emotional material

 I spent years with a jaw that clicked and popped like a broken typewriter. My dentist told me I was grinding my teeth...
htmlContent:
Your jaw is not just for chewing

I spent years with a jaw that clicked and popped like a broken typewriter. My dentist told me I was grinding my teeth at night. He gave me a mouthguard and sent me on my way. But the tension never left. It just moved deeper.

Here's the thing about TMJ disorders. They're rarely just about the mechanics of your jaw joint. Your temporomandibular joint sits right at the crossroads of your nervous system, your breath, your voice, and your survival responses. When that joint locks up, when the muscles around it knot into steel cables, when your teeth clench so hard you wake up with headaches, your body is trying to tell you something.

Something you haven't said out loud.

Something you maybe can't say out loud.

I'm not talking about some new age theory. I'm talking about what researchers like Stephen Porges have spent decades mapping out with Polyvagal Theory. Porges showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger, and that our facial muscles, our jaw, our middle ear muscles are all part of that scanning system. Your jaw is literally wired into your survival brain.

Think about what your jaw does when you're scared. It clenches. When you're angry and can't speak, your teeth press together. When you're holding back tears, your throat tightens and your jaw locks. When you have to be polite while someone disrespects you, your molars grind. Every unspoken word, every swallowed scream, every forced smile gets stored in that joint.

Your jaw is the gatekeeper of your voice. And when that gate stays locked, the tension accumulates.

The mouth as a storage unit for survival

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma gets stored in the body. Not as memories in your head, but as physical tension patterns in your tissues. Your jaw is one of the primary places this happens. Why? Because the mouth is one of the first places we learn to control ourselves.

As babies, we cry when we need something. We scream when we're uncomfortable. We use our mouths to signal distress. But somewhere along the way, most of us learned that our voices aren't safe. We learned that speaking up gets us punished. That crying gets us dismissed. That telling the truth about how we feel gets us rejected.

So we close our mouths. We tighten our jaws. We hold the words in.

And then we wonder why our teeth hurt, why our necks ache, why we get tension headaches that feel like someone tightened a vice around our skulls.

I see this pattern constantly in people with complex trauma histories. They have TMJ issues that no dentist can fully resolve. They've tried mouthguards, chiropractic adjustments, Botox injections. And those things help a little, sure. But the tension keeps coming back because the underlying cause hasn't been addressed.

The underlying cause is that your nervous system is still in survival mode. It's still bracing for the next threat. It's still keeping your jaw locked because somewhere, deep down, your body believes that if you relax that muscle, something dangerous will come out.

A scream. A sob. A truth that someone didn't want to hear.

Your body is protecting you from that release. But the protection itself has become the problem.

What your jaw knows that you don't

I started paying attention to my jaw about three years ago. Not just when it hurt, but when I felt calm. When I felt angry. When I felt anxious. I noticed that my jaw would tighten the moment I started thinking about something I didn't want to feel. It was like a reflex. A micro-movement that happened before I even registered the emotion.

This is where the nervous system science gets really interesting. Dan Siegel talks about the window of tolerance, the zone where your nervous system can handle life without going into fight, flight, freeze, or collapse. When you're outside that window, your body starts doing things automatically. Clenching your jaw is one of those things.

Your jaw tightens to help you regulate. It's a form of bracing. It's like your body's way of saying, "I can't handle what's happening right now, so I'm going to hold everything together by force."

But here's the problem. That bracing becomes chronic. Your nervous system gets stuck in that pattern. And then your jaw stays tight even when there's no threat. Even when you're just sitting on the couch watching TV. Even when you're trying to sleep.

This is why a mouthguard alone isn't enough. It stops your teeth from grinding against each other, but it doesn't address the nervous system that's driving the grinding. It's like putting a bandaid on a leaky pipe without turning off the water.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). It walks through exactly how to start noticing these patterns and working with them rather than against them.

The link between suppressed speech and jaw tension

There's a reason your jaw gets tight when you're in a conversation where you can't speak freely. Think about the last time someone said something that bothered you and you didn't respond. What happened in your jaw? What happened in your throat?

For most people, the answer is tightening. Maybe you felt a lump in your throat. Maybe your jaw pushed forward slightly. Maybe you realized you were holding your breath.

This is your body preparing to speak and then stopping itself. It's a freeze response, but a specific one. It's the freeze of your voice. And every time you do this, every time you swallow an unspoken word, you add a little more tension to that system.

Over years, decades, that tension builds. It becomes structural. It changes the way your jaw sits, the way your neck aligns, the way your shoulders carry themselves. Your whole posture starts to reflect the things you haven't said.

I'm not saying every TMJ issue is caused by suppressed emotion. There are structural problems, injuries, and genetic factors that play a role. But for a huge number of people, especially those with trauma histories, the emotional component is the missing piece that nobody talks about.

Your dentist won't ask you about your childhood. Your orthodontist won't ask about the time you were told to stop crying or else. Your physical therapist won't ask what you wanted to say but couldn't. And so you keep treating the symptom without touching the source.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). It helps relax muscles and can support the nervous system in ways that make it easier to notice what's actually going on in your body.

How to start releasing the jaw without forcing it

You can't just decide to relax your jaw. If you've been clenching for years, your body doesn't know how to let go. The muscles have shortened. The neural pathways have been reinforced. Your nervous system thinks clenching is normal.

So you have to approach this slowly. Gently. With curiosity instead of force.

Start by just noticing. Not trying to change anything, just noticing. When does your jaw tighten? Is it when you're working? When you're thinking about a difficult conversation? When you're trying to fall asleep? When you're around certain people?

Notice the pattern without judging it. Your jaw is doing what it learned to do to keep you safe. Thank it for that. Then start to offer it alternatives.

One thing that helped me was putting my awareness on my jaw throughout the day. Just a soft check-in. "Hey jaw, what's happening right now?" Most of the time, it was clenched. And just noticing it, without forcing it to relax, would create a tiny opening. A millimeter of space. A breath.

From there, you can experiment. Can you soften your jaw just a little? Can you let your tongue rest on the roof of your mouth instead of pressing against your teeth? Can you take a breath and imagine the exhale coming through your jaw, releasing some of that tension?

This isn't about fixing yourself. It's about building a relationship with a part of your body that's been holding a heavy load for a long time.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is the TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Using it on your upper back and neck can help release some of the tension that feeds into your jaw.

I also use a weighted blanket sometimes, especially at night when my jaw tends to clench more. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is the YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The weight can help your nervous system feel more grounded, which sometimes translates into less clenching.

But here's what I want you to understand. This work is not linear. You might notice your jaw softening, and then a stressful event happens, and it locks up again. That's not failure. That's your nervous system doing its job. The goal is not to never clench again. The goal is to have more choice, more awareness, more capacity to release when the threat passes.

Sit with that for a moment. Your jaw has been holding so much for so long. It deserves your attention, your patience, your compassion. Not your frustration.

And as you start to release the tension, you might find that other things start to release too. Words you've been holding. Tears you've been swallowing. Truths you've been protecting yourself from speaking.

Your jaw is not just a joint. It's a doorway. And when you start to open it, you never know what might want to come through.

That's the scary part. And that's also the beautiful part.
